notes: Grade the Mikado Method DECISIONS: revert-on-discovery (keep the prerequisite learning, throw away the half-done attempt), leaf-first ordering (only nodes whose prerequisites are all done are actionable), re-implement-don't-cherry-pick (implement a ready node fresh on a clean base, not by salvaging the reverted spike), and reactive splitting of a sprawled branch into independently-mergeable nodes. The naive answer (no skill) tends to: power through the breakage, start at the goal, salvage the spike branch, or keep pushing one giant branch. Inline Q&A; read-only harness. 2026-07-18: documented the engine's dangling-dep/undefined-node refusal in the skill body (it previously documented only cycle-errors), which is the fact eval-4 grades — closing the eval-gap with a real teach, not a manufactured case. De-leaked the eval-4/eval-5 prompts (dropped the literal .mikado/plan.md path + 'mikado-graph approach' trigger) which reduces explicit cue leakage (ambient auto-trigger of the installed skill can still occur — the delta is candidate-over-installed, and eval-4 discriminates on the newly-documented refusal behavior regardless). Engine now also rejects a typo'd status (was a silent wrong-READY). 6 cases retained: 4 map 1:1 to the documented decisions + 2 engine.

evals.name: revert-on-discovery
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. I started a big refactor; partway in it breaks three call sites that each need their own prep first. Using the Mikado Method, state exactly what to do with my current half-done changes and how to record what I learned.
assertions: Records each newly-discovered prerequisite as a child node of the change being attempted
REVERTS the half-done attempt (the Mikado move) rather than pushing through the breakage
Keeps the learning (the graph/prerequisites), not the half-done code
Plans to do the prerequisites first, then re-attempt the original change

evals.name: leaf-first-only-ready
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT plan or run tools, just answer. My Mikado graph: goal depends on A and B; A depends on C; B and C have no unmet prerequisites. Using the method, state which node(s) I should implement first and why, and whether I can start on the goal now.
assertions: Says to start with the leaves B and C (nodes whose prerequisites are all done), in any order
Does NOT start the goal (it has unmet prerequisites A and B)
Notes A becomes actionable only after C is done; goal only after A and B
Frames only leaf/ready nodes as actionable, enforced by the dependency order

evals.name: reimplement-not-cherrypick
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT plan or run tools, just answer. A prerequisite node just became ready (all its deps are done). I still have the messy exploratory branch from when I first discovered this prerequisite. Using the Mikado Method, state how I implement the now-ready node.
assertions: Implements the ready node FRESH on a clean/current base
Does NOT cherry-pick or salvage the reverted exploratory/spike branch
Treats the spike code as throwaway and the graph as the durable artifact
Makes it an independently-mergeable commit

evals.name: reactive-split-sprawled-branch
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT plan or run tools, just answer. I have a branch that sprawled into 40 files and won't merge cleanly. Using the Mikado Method reactively, state how to turn it into something mergeable.
assertions: Factors the sprawl into a dependency graph of smaller nodes (what could merge on its own; what blocks what)
Re-implements each node cleanly on main rather than forcing the giant branch through
Each node is independently mergeable; a node that can't merge alone has a hidden prerequisite to add
Abandons/throws away the sprawled branch as the source of truth (the graph + fresh commits replace it)

evals.name: dangling-dep-engine-flags-undefined
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. I'm working a big change as a dependency graph of prerequisite steps. One step, "add-tests", lists a prerequisite "ci-setup", but there is no step called "ci-setup" anywhere in my graph (I think I renamed it). How do I determine what's actionable, and what do I do about "ci-setup"?
assertions: Reaches for the bundled engine (running mikado.py on the plan) as the authority for what's actionable (a no-skill answer wouldn't know a bundled engine exists), rather than eyeballing the graph or manually tracing readiness
Identifies ci-setup as an UNDEFINED/nonexistent node (a dangling reference, almost certainly the rename typo), NOT an ordinary unmet prerequisite that just needs doing

States the engine treats a dependency on an undefined node as invalid input — it refuses the plan with an explicit error (e.g. 'depends on undefined node "ci-setup"') and exits nonzero, rather than silently listing add-tests as blocked-forever
Fix is to correct the dangling reference (repoint the dep id or add the missing ci-setup node), NOT to 'go do ci-setup'

evals.name: cycle-engine-errors-nothing-ready
evals.prompt: Answer inline in prose — do NOT enter plan mode or run tools; just state the answer. In my prerequisite graph, step A depends on step B and step B depends on step A (both unfinished). How do I determine which steps are actionable right now, and what do I do about A and B?
assertions: Reaches for the bundled engine (mikado.py on the plan) to classify readiness, per the skill's 'ask the engine, don't eyeball' rule (a no-skill answer wouldn't invoke a bundled engine)
Identifies A↔B as a dependency CYCLE — neither can ever be ready because each blocks the other
States the engine specifically DETECTS the cycle and reports it as an error (e.g. a '✗ CYCLE' report) exiting nonzero, rather than printing a normal READY/BLOCKED classification
No node is actionable until the cycle is broken by removing/repointing the mis-modeled edge (a genuine prerequisite relationship is never mutual) — not by arbitrarily starting one
